contents: Kawasaki Disease Complicated With Macrophage ActivationSyndrome: A Systematic ReviewSusana Garci'a-Pavo'n, MD,* Marco A. Yamazaki-Nakashimada, MD,wMilton Ba'ez, MD,z Karla L. Borjas-Aguilar, MD,w and Chiharu Murata, MScySummary: Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS), also known assecondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, is a rare and potentiallyfatal complication of Kawasaki disease (KD). We report 2 cases,performed a literature search, and analyze the characteristics of MASassociated with KD. A total of 69 patients were evaluated, 34 reportedthe date of the diagnosis of MAS and KD, 6% had a diagnosis of MASbefore KD, 21% had a simultaneous presentation, and 73% had thediagnosis of MAS after KD. Different treatment approaches wereobserved with corticosteroids administered in 87%, cyclosporine in49%, etoposide (VP-16) in 39%, and monoclonal anti-TNF in 6% ofcases. Coronary abnormalities were especially high in this group ofpatients (46%) and 9 patients died (13%). The persistence of feverwith splenomegaly, hyperferritinemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevatedaspartate aminotransferase (AST) should prompt the consideration ofMAS complicating KD.Key Words: Kawasaki disease, macrophage activation syndrome,secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, hyperferritinemia

(J Pediatr Hematol Oncol 2017;00:000-000)Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute vasculitis predominantlyoccurring in children from 6 months to 5 years characterizedby fever, conjunctivitis, polymorphous exanthema, crackedlips, changes in the extremities, and cervical lymphadenopathywith a predisposition to involve coronary arteries. Macrophageactivation syndrome (MAS), which is also known as secondaryhemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, is a rare and potentiallyfatal complication of KD.1 MAS is an inflammatory reactiongenerated by excessive cytokine production, macrophageactivation, and hemophagocytosis. MAS mainly presentswith prolonged fever, rash, hepatosplenomegaly, and hall-mark features that include liver dysfunction, elevated lactatedehydrogenase (LDH), coagulopathy, hypofibrinogenemia,hyperferritinemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and pancytopenia.1Among rheumatic disorders, MAS occurs most fre-quently in Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA) andhas a 10% mortality risk.1 In recent years, this syndromehas been increasingly reported in patients with otherrheumatic disorders such as KD. We report 2 patients withKD complicated by MAS and a review of the literaturewith descriptive analysis of the clinical characteristics andtherapeutic options.2-33

MATERIALS AND METHODSWe report 2 unpublished cases of MAS and KD. Inaddition, a Pubmed search was performed combining theterms(hemophagocyticsyndrome,hemophagocytosis,macrophage activation syndrome, and Kawasaki disease).This study was performed and reported according to thePRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews. Relevant arti-cles about MAS-KD were collected. Additional referenceswere obtained from PsyINFO, Cochrane library, LILACS,Elsevier, Springer, KoreaMed, and WHO using the com-bined terms MeSH and searching key words with the terms;(macrophage activation syndrome OR "Macrophage Acti-vation Syndrome" [MeSH]) OR (Hemophagocytic Lym-phohistiocytosis [MeSH] or hemophagocytic syndrome)AND (Kawasaki disease or Mucocutaneous lymph nodesyndrome) up to September 2016. A nonfilter for languageand date was used in the search. Case reports and caseseries of MAS-KD were included with the available infor-mation, as were consistent clinical manifestations and adefinite diagnosis.The strategy is detailed as follows:TermsResults1. "Macrophage Activation Syndrome"[Mesh]1742. Macrophage activation syndrome16783. #1 OR #216784. "Lymphohistiocytosis, Hemophagocytic"[Mesh]15615. Hemophagocytic syndrome32636. Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis22537. #4 OR #5 OR #632638. "Mucocutaneous Lymph Node Syndrome"[Mesh]48169. Kawasaki disease883210. #8 OR #9883211. #3 OR #7 AND #1060Data BaseResultsAnalyzed Papers

hepatosplenomegaly was found. A complete blood countand laboratory tests revealed Hb, 8.9 g/dL; white blood cell(WBC), 5300/mL; neutrophils, 4200/mL; lymphocytes, 1100/mL;platelets, 87,000/mm3; ferritin, 567ng/mL (that increased to820ng/mL on the 13th day); lactate dehydrogenase (LDH),768IU/L; aspartate aminotransferase (AST), 91IU/L; alanineaminotransferase (ALT), 124IU/L; gammaglutamyl trans-ferase, 236IU/L; total bilirubin (TB), 2.0mg/dL; fibrinogen,78mg/dL; triglycerides, 299mg/dL; and erythrosedimentationrate (ESR), 40mm/h. A thoracoabdominal computed tomog-raphy scan was performed, and a tumor located in the poste-rior mediastinum was observed, suggesting a cystic image inaddition to hydrops of the gallbladder. Morbilliform exan-thema seemed to affect the head, trunk, extremities, and pal-moplantar areas, and edema of the feet was observed. Thepatient presented with pharyngeal hyperemia, fissured lips, anderythema at the Bacille Calmette-Guerin (BCG) immunizationsite. Treatment for KD was started with intravenous immu-noglobulins (IVIG) (2g/kg), acetylsalicylic acid (80mg/kg/d),and intravenous methylprednisolone (30mg/kg/d) for 3 con-secutive days with resolution of the fever. The EKG andechocardiogram were unremarkable, bone marrow aspirationshowed 1-2 hemophagocytic cells per slide. The patient devel-oped fine periungual desquamation and edema of the feet andperineal erythema. On day 13, he developed fever (38.81C),blood cultures were positive for Klebsiella pneumoniae, andthere was an increase in acute phase reactants and the patientdeveloped a pleural effusion. Aminotransferases and ferritinwere elevated and a second dose of IVIG (2mg/kg/dose) wasadministered. A coronary computed angiotomography scan ofthe coronary arteries showed a coronary artery ectasia andaneurysm and anticoagulation with subcutaneous enoxaparinwas started. Infliximab was administered because of poorresponse to conventional treatment. A computed tomographyangiography of the chest and abdomen revealed systemicarterial aneurysms in the left subclavian, splenic, renal, and iliacarteries (Fig. 1). Cyclosporine (5mg/kg/d) was started; afollow-up computed tomography angiography demonstratedresolution of the hygromas and aneurysms (Fig. 2).

Case 2A 16-year-old male presented with a 2-week history offever, maculopapular rash, and arthralgias. The physicalexamination revealed cheilitis, cervical lymphadenopathy,generalized rash, conjunctival erythema, and edema of thehands. Laboratory tests showed Hb, 13.6 g/dL; WBC,26,200/mL; neutrophils, 24,100/mL; lymphocytes, 800/mL;platelets, 329,000/mm3; negative HIV; and parvovirus B19and a positive IgM EBV. An echocardiogram revealedpericarditis without coronary abnormalities. IVIG wasadministered; however, he had persistent fever and shockand was admitted to the intensive care unit. The patientdeveloped desquamation of the fingertips and feet andhemophagocytic syndrome was suspected. Laboratory testsshowedferritin,121,527 ng/mL;Hb,9.5 g/dL;WBC,35,000/mL; neutrophils, 33,900/mL; lymphocytes, 704/mL;platelets, 188,000/mm3; LDH, 626 IU/L; AST, 90 IU/L;ALT, 250 IU/L; gammaglutamyl transferase, 567 IU/L;total bilirubin (TB) 2.5 mg/dL; fibrinogen, 329 mg/dL; tri-glycerides, 315 mg/dL; a bone marrow aspiration revealed 2hemophagocytic cells. Methylprednisolone pulses startedwith clinical improvement, the patient continued treatmentwith oral prednisone and cyclosporine. Five days afterdischarge, he was readmitted with a fever and generalizedrash, labwork showed ferritin, 40,460 ng/mL; AST, 51 IU/L; ALT, 75 IU/L; albumin, 2.9; triglycerides, 277 mgdL;ESR, 51 mm/h; Hb, 11.9 g/dL; WBC, 9400/mL; neutrophils,7200/mL; lymphocytes, 800/mL; and platelets, 329,000/mm3.Treatment with a combination of cyclosporine, IVIG,methotrexate, and corticosteroids achieved resolution ofsymptoms and labwork abnormalities.From the literature search, 67 patients were identi-fied.2-33 Of the 227 articles identified, 32 articles on MAS-KD were eligible for the study. The earliest report was in1995 by Ohga and colleagues, and the most recent studywas in 2016 by Dogan and colleagues, 3 case series reporteda total of 32 patients.3,18,22

PatientsOf the 69 cases analyzed, the mean age at diagnosiswas 5.6 years (range, 7 weeks old to 17 years old), themajority of the cases were male 68% (47/69), and Asianethnicity was the most frequently reported (75%). A totalof 77% of the patients presented complete KD (Table 1).Of the 69 analyzed cases, 34 reported the date of thediagnosis of KD and MAS, with the diagnosis of MASFIGURE 1. Thoracic angiotomography axial reconstructions. A, Ectasia of the proximal and medial segments of the LAD and theproximal segment of the Cx. B, Remission of coronary ectasia. Cx indicates circumflex artery; LAD, left anterior descending artery.made before the diagnosis of KD in 6% (2/34), withsimultaneous presentation in 21% (7/34), and 73% (25/34)of patients with diagnosis of MAS after the diagnosis ofKD.Clinical Manifestations and Laboratory ResultsIn 24 of 68 patients ESR was reported, 3 of 24 patientshad normal ESR and the rest had elevated ESR, hypofi-brinogenemia was found in 38% of the patients (21/55)(Table 2) and AST was elevated in 94% (30/32) of thepatients. Ferritin was elevated in 95% (63/66) of the cases,of the 59 cases with fever and ferritin >684ng/mL, 73%

fulfilled the 2016 MAS-sJIA consensus criteria34-36 and in69% (45/65) of the patients splenomegaly was found.Hemophagocytosis in the bone marrow, lymph nodes,spleen, or the liver was found in 88% (51/58) of patients, in11 patients, bone marrow aspiration was not performed. Interms of potential infectious triggers or perpetuating agentsof MAS; Candida, Aspergillus, Klebsiella, Streptococcus andEBVpneumonia,tonsillitisandbacteremiawerereported.4-7,33Tensinglecasereportsevaluatedinterleukins(ILs) levels, with high levels of IL-1, IL-8, IL-10, andinterferon-g,whereastumornecrosisfactor(TNF)-a,IL-1b, IL-2R, IL-2, IL-4, and IL-6 levels were notalways elevated.4,5,7,8,11,20,26,29,30,33 Familial hemophago-cytic lymphohistiocytosis (FHLH)-related genetic muta-tions were studied in 6 reports and no mutations werefound.5,11,14,17,18,30 Cummings et al5 reported low levels ofperforin and NK-cells but PRF1, MUNC-13-4 and XLP(SH2D1A) mutations were negative, and Shafferman et al17reported low levels of perforin and granzyme-B in CD8T-cells and NK cells but no mutations in STXBP2, STX11,MUNC-13-4 or RAB27A.Four patients were diagnosed with sepsis and 6 wereadmitted to the intensive care unit because of cardiaccomplications. The average hospitalization time was 17days, with a minimum of 4 days and a maximum of 60 days.Amongthecomplications,46%(32/69)hadcardiacFIGURE 2. Thorax-abdomen-pelvis angiotomography. Volumetric reconstruction technique. A, Arterial stenosis of the left common iliacartery associated with right common iliac artery ectasia and both internal iliac branches. B, Remission of stenosis and bilateral iliacarterial vascular ectasia.TABLE 1. Demographic Features of the 69 CasesVariablesn/Total (%)

Age (median [IQR, min max]) (y)5.6 (7, 0.13 y 17)Sex (male)47/69 (68)Ethnic groupAsian30/40 (75)Hispanic4/40 (10)African2/40 (5)White2/40 (5)Indian2/40 (5)Classic KD44/57 (77)IQR indicates interquartile range; KD, Kawasaki disease.manifestations, 13% (9/69) had CNS complications, and9% (6/69) had pneumonia. Of the 32 patients with cardiacmanifestations all presented with coronary abnormalities,3% (1/32) with ventricular dilatation, 6% (2/32) withvalvular abnormalities, and 12% (4/32) with pericardialeffusion.DiagnosisAll the studies used the HLH-2004 criteria to diagnoseMAS with the exception of Wang et al22 who reported 8cases using the Ravelli's 2005 preliminary criteria for MASin sJIA.34 Seventy-eight percent (54/69) of the patientsfulfilled HLH-2004 criteria and 74% (51/69) fulfilled the2016 consensus criteria for MAS-sJIA (Table 3). Hemo-phagocytosis, a criterion included in the HLH-2004 criteriaand absent in the 2016 consensus criteria for MAS-sJIA,was present in 88% (51/58) of the patients. The frequencyof hyperferritinemia was 95% (63/66) with a cut-off levelZ500 mg/L (HLH-2004 criteria) and 92% (61/66) with acut-off level Z684 ng/L (2016 consensus criteria MAS-sJIA).35-38 In only 5 patients sCD25 was investigated, in allof them the level was elevated.7,11,29,30,33 It is important to

notice that the 2016 consensus criteria for MAS weredeveloped for the diagnosis of MAS in sJIA and notfor KD.TreatmentAll patients received IVIG, 10% received 1 dose and90% received Z2 doses for the treatment of MAS-KD.Different approaches were observed, with corticosteroidsadministered in 87% (60/69), cyclosporine in 49% (34/69),VP-16 in 39% (27/69), monoclonal anti-TNF in 5.7%(4/69), and IL-1 receptor antagonist in 4.3% (3/69)(Table 4). Twenty-four patients received the HLH-2004protocol. Nine patients died (13%), 7 of them were treatedwith the HLH-2004 protocol. Of the 9 deceased patients,2 died from acute myocardial infarction, 3 from pneumonia,1 from sepsis, and in 3 patients the cause was notspecified.4,11,18,21,22,24,32

DISCUSSIONOur literature search identified 67 patients with KDwith MAS from 14 different countries. Asian patients andAsian reports were overrepresented in this study; possibleexplanations are the high prevalence of KD in this regionbecause of a genetic susceptibility. Interestingly, the inci-dence of EBV-HLH is relatively high in Asian countries,indicating the importance of a genetic background in thepathogenesis of EBV-HLH.39MAS is a relatively infrequent complication in rheu-matology. The incidence of MAS in children with KD hasbeen estimated to be 1.1%.22 Latino and colleaguesreported an incidence of 1.9% (12/638) in patients withMAS and KD.2 Atteritano et al1 reported 25 patientsbetween 1990 and 2012 and found a 7-fold higher risk ofMAS in KD among patients older than 5 years old. In ouranalysis, there was a wide range in age, from infants toteenagers, with a peak age between 1 and 2 years old.Interestingly, 34 of the 69 patients were older than 5 yearsof age, suggesting that older age could be a predisposingfactor for MAS, as reported by Kato et al.33MAS can result in progressive multiorgan failure and fataloutcomes if not recognized, early recognition of MAS is oftenchallenging given the lack of a single pathognomonic clinical orlaboratory features. The clinical findings and laboratory tests ofMAS and KD overlap; furthermore, histopathologic featuresTABLE 2. Treatment and DeathsTreatmentTotal = 69(n [%])Deceased (n/Total)(% [95% CI])IntravenousImmunoglobulins69 (100)9/69 (13 [5%-20%])G-CSF3 (4)1/3 (33 [6%-79%])Anti-IL-1r3 (4)0/3 (0 [0%-66%])Anti-TNF-a4 (5.7)0/4 (0 [0%-49%])CI indicates confidence interval; G-CSF, granulocyte-colony stimulatingfactor; IL, interleukin; TNF, tumor necrosis factor.Garci'a-Pavo'n et alJ Pediatr Hematol Oncol  Volume 00, Number 00, '' 2017

of hemophagocytosis may not be present during the initialstages. Persistent fever, rash, thrombocytopenia, and elevationof transaminases, can be observed in patients with IVIGresistant KD without MAS, hypertriglyceridemia in KDwithout MAS has also been described.40ESR, a marker of inflammation, is affected by plasmaconstituents, particularly fibrinogen in KD, a state ofhyperfibrinogenemia caused by inflammation has beendescribed.40 A normal or a drop in ESR levels could be asign of MAS as a reflection of hypofibrinogenemia. In ourreview, only 38% presented hypofibrinogenemia and themajority of patients had elevated ESR levels, these findingsmake ESR unreliable in the diagnosis of MAS in KD.MAS can be observed in any stage of KD (acute,subacute, or convalescence), it can develop before thediagnosis of KD, but most frequently, it develops simul-taneously. MAS is usually not diagnosed at an early stage,thus leading to a poor prognosis. Kang et al18 suggest thatthe MAS-KD diagnosis must be considered when recurrentKD develops in the first month of the disease. Recently,Choi et al41 suggested to consider MAS in all refractory KDcases ("Occult MAS"). Nasir et al42 reported 3 newbornswith KD and very high levels of ferritin (7065 pmol/L,33,372 pmol/L, and 17304 pmol/L) who had adequateresponse to IVIG and acetylsalicylic acid. They were treatedpromptly (before the fifth day of the disease) suggestingthat they may have had early stage of MAS.Thrombocytosis is a nonspecific characteristic featureof KD; however, thrombocytopenia can be present in 2%of KD patients. Thrombocytopenia has been considered asa risk factor for a more severe disease, associated with acutemyocardial infarction and coronary artery aneurysms.43 Inour review, 87% patients (42/48) presented with thrombo-cytopenia, the majority of the patients had moderatethrombocytopenia and only 3 patients presented withsevere thrombocytopenia.Splenomegaly was reported in 69% of patients (45/65)and it is a useful clinical sign of MAS in KD, as spleno-megaly is rare in KD. Splenomegaly is included in theHLH-2004 criteria38 but not in the 2016 consensus criteriafor MAS in sJIA as splenomegaly is a common feature ofsJIA without MAS.35 A high index of suspicion is requiredin KD progressing to MAS, especially when thrombocy-topenia, elevated AST, and ferritin are present. Coronaryabnormalities were especially high in this group of patients(46%) compared with 15% to 25% of untreated KDpatients without MAS. Mortality was high (13%), similarto MAS complicating sJIA.The pathophysiology of MAS is poorly understood,whereas in FHL a cytotoxic defect is known to be impor-tant.37 Central to HLH/MAS pathogenesis is a cytokinestorm with markedly increased levels of numerous proin-flammatory cytokines. In few patients with cytokine meas-urements, a high INF-gamma level has been identified, thiscytokine plays a central role in FHL. Identification and val-idation of clinically relevant biomarkers is essential to detectthis complication in KD patients. There is a subset of patientsclassified as MAS-like disease because they did not have 5 ofthe 8 required criteria; in these patients the activity of NKcells and IL-2Rs determination may be of value.Decreased NK cell function or perforin expressionabnormalities,withpolymorphismsinUNC13Dandmutations in PFR1 were reported in patients with sJIA andMAS.44-46 There are few studies of NK cells and cytotoxicCD8 lymphocytes in KD; it has been found that CD8

lymphocytes infiltrating the coronary artery do not expressthe cytotoxic proteins perforin and granzyme.47 The levelsof NK cells have been reported to be lower than normalchildren. NKG2D is expressed constitutively on essentiallyall resting human NK cells and CD8 + T cells. NKG2Dserves as a general sensor for recognizing the "induced self"fordetectingandeliminatinghyperproliferativecells,transformed cells, or cells infected by pathogens. Ge et al48reported that the levels of NKG2D expression on NK cellsand CD8(+) T cells in the acute phase of KD were sig-nificantly lower than those in normal controls (P < 0.05),and the levels of NKG2D expression in patients with cor-onary artery lesion (KD-coronary artery lesions (CAL)(+))were lower than those in patients without coronary arterylesion KD-CAL(). A defect in NKG2D pathway may berelevant in MAS associated with KD. In all the patientswith KD and MAS, a genetic factor leading to this com-plication has not been found.Therapeutic options in the treatment of resistant KD(without MAS) include additional IVIG, corticosteroids,plasmapheresis, methotrexate, TNF inhibitors, cyclosporine,and IL-1 blockers.49 There is no uniform treatment for MAS-KD, as sometimes it responds to corticosteroids alone or incombination with immunosuppressant drugs or other thera-pies such as granulocyte-colony stimulating factor, TNFainhibitors and anti-IL1 antibodies. However, MAS can betriggered by some of these medications, and frequently, thereis an underlying infection precipitating KD.There is no doubt that the HLH-2004 protocol fortreating FHLH can be life-saving, but less intensive anti-inflammatory treatment is frequently sufficient for patientswith other forms of hemophagocytic inflammatory diseaseand aggressive immunosuppression may even be contra-indicated.50 In our review, of 24 patients in which the HLH-2004 protocol was used, 7 died with a mortality of 29%.The pathogenesis of MAS might differ from FHLH andtherefore the therapeutic approach may be different.Miettunen et al16 suggest treatment with IVIG, cyclo-sporine, IL-1 receptor antagonist and steroids for MAS-KD according to the severity over the HLH-2004 protocolbecause of the potential risk of sepsis and malignancy.Although plasmapheresis has been used successfully inrefractory KD and in non-KD MAS, this therapeuticoption has not been explored in MAS-KD.50,51 Cyclo-sporine, in contrast, has been used effectively in IVIG-resistant KD and in MAS associated with sJIA and is alsoan essential component of the HLH-2004 protocol. In ourreview, it was used in 49% (34/69) of the patients.52,53 In 5patients monoclonal antibodies were administered (IL-1receptor antagonist, anti-TNFa, or the combination ofboth) with good response. Anakinra, an IL-1 receptorantagonist, has been reported as a promising therapeuticoption for MAS.16,54 Corticosteroids and IVIG can be usedas a first-line treatment with cyclosporine, monoclonalantibodies, or plasmapheresis as add-on alternatives.This study analyzed all the cases published regardingthis rare complication; however, the number still too smallto draw firm conclusions. Diagnostic criteria that can helpto identify etiologically different conditions are needed tooffer disease-specific treatment.

CONCLUSIONSThe ideal diagnostic approach or treatment for MAS-KD has not been defined, and current diagnostic criteria forJ Pediatr Hematol Oncol  Volume 00, Number 00, '' 2017Kawasaki Disease: A Systematic ReviewMAS in KD do not exist. The persistence of fever withsplenomegaly, hyperferritinemia, thrombocytopenia, andelevated AST should prompt the consideration of MAScomplicating KD. A combined therapeutic approach withcorticosteroids, IVIG, cyclosporine, and monoclonal anti-bodies as the first-line of treatment is suggested. The HLH-2004 protocol and plasmapheresis are valid alternatives forsevere refractory cases.ACKNOWLEDGMENTSTo Cecilia Solis Galicia for her technical assistance andRogerio Santiago Herrera, MD Radiologist of the GeneralNaval Hospital of High Speciality for the edition of thefeatured images.REFERENCES
